description: Early blight is one of the most common tomato diseases worldwide, causing significant yield losses if left untreated. It is caused by a fungus that thrives in warm, humid conditions.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings forming a target-like pattern
Yellowing (chlorosis) of tissue surrounding lesions
Lower and older leaves affected first, moving upward
Lesions may merge, causing entire leaves to turn yellow and die
Dark, sunken lesions may also appear on stems and fruit
causes: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Spreads through infected plant debris and soil. Favored by warm temperatures (24â€“29Â°C), high humidity, and prolonged leaf wetness.

organic: Remove and destroy all infected leaves immediately
Apply copper-based fungicide (copper oxychloride) every 7â€“10 days
Spray neem oil solution (2 tablespoons per litre of water) on affected areas
Improve air circulation by pruning lower leaves
chemical: Apply Chlorothalonil (Daconil) at label rate, repeat every 7 days
Use Mancozeb (Dithane M-45) as a protective spray before disease spreads
Azoxystrobin (Amistar) for systemic control of established infections
prevention: Rotate crops â€” avoid planting tomatoes in the same spot for 2â€“3 years
Water plants at the base; avoid wetting foliage
Use disease-resistant tomato varieties (e.g., Mountain Supreme)
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash onto leaves
Space plants adequately (60cm apart) for good air circulation

description: Late blight is one of the most destructive plant diseases in history â€” it caused the Irish Potato Famine. It can destroy an entire tomato crop within days under favourable conditions.
symptoms: Large, water-soaked, irregularly shaped dark green to brown lesions on leaves
White fuzzy mould (sporulation) visible on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Brown, firm lesions on stems that may girdle and kill the plant
Fruit shows large, brown, greasy-looking rot
Rapid collapse of entire plant in severe cases
causes: Caused by the oomycete (water mould) Phytophthora infestans. Thrives in cool (10â€“20Â°C), wet, and humid weather. Spreads rapidly through airborne spores.

organic: Remove and burn all infected plant parts immediately â€” do not compost
Apply copper hydroxide spray (e.g., Kocide) every 5â€“7 days
Spray potassium bicarbonate solution to raise pH on leaf surfaces
Apply Bacillus subtilis biological fungicide as a preventive
chemical: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) â€” highly effective systemic fungicide
Use Cymoxanil (Curzate) combined with a contact fungicide
Dimethomorph (Acrobat) for resistant strains â€” rotate with other modes of action
prevention: Plant certified disease-free transplants from reputable nurseries
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip irrigation at soil level
Monitor weather forecasts â€” apply preventive sprays before rainy periods
Destroy all plant debris at the end of season
Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Iron Lady, Mountain Magic)

description: Bacterial spot causes significant defoliation and fruit spotting in tomatoes. It is especially destructive in warm, wet climates typical of tropical and sub-tropical farming regions.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn dark brown with yellow halos
Spots may have a greasy or water-soaked appearance initially
Severe defoliation leading to sunscald on exposed fruit
Raised, blister-like spots on fruit that darken and become scabby
Dark, sunken lesions on stems
causes: Caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria bacteria. Spreads through rain splash, infected transplants, contaminated tools, and irrigation water. Favoured by temperatures of 24â€“30Â°C with high humidity.

organic: Apply copper-based bactericide (copper sulphate) at first sign of disease
Remove and destroy heavily infected plant material
Avoid working with plants when foliage is wet to prevent spread
Use garlic extract or onion extract spray as a natural bactericide
chemical: Copper hydroxide + Mancozeb combination sprays every 5â€“7 days
Streptomycin sulphate spray (where legally permitted in your country)
Acibenzolar-S-methyl (Actigard) as a plant defence activator
prevention: Purchase certified disease-free transplants only
Rotate crops â€” do not plant tomatoes or peppers in the same area for 2 years
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach solution between plants
Avoid overhead watering â€” use drip irrigation
Plant resistant varieties where available

description: Tomato leaf mold primarily affects greenhouse and high-tunnel tomatoes, though it also occurs outdoors in humid climates. While rarely fatal, it can cause significant yield reduction through defoliation.
symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grey velvety mould growth on the underside of affected areas
Infected leaves curl, yellow, and drop prematurely
In severe cases, flowers and young fruit may be affected
No stem or fruit lesions typically â€” mainly a foliar disease
causes: Caused by the fungus Passalora fulva (formerly Cladosporium fulvum). Thrives in high humidity (>85%) and moderate temperatures (22â€“25Â°C). Common in enclosed growing environments.

organic: Increase ventilation in greenhouses to reduce humidity below 85%
Remove and dispose of infected leaves
Apply copper-based fungicide sprays
Use potassium bicarbonate spray as an anti-fungal measure
chemical: Apply Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb as protective sprays
Trifloxystrobin (Flint) for systemic control
Rotate fungicides to prevent resistance build-up
prevention: Maintain greenhouse relative humidity below 85% at all times
Space plants properly for good air circulation
Water in the morning so foliage dries during the day
Use resistant varieties (e.g., Dombito, Virosa)
Avoid working in crops when leaves are wet

description: Your tomato plant appears healthy! No signs of disease detected. Continue your current management practices to keep your crop thriving.
symptoms: Deep green, firm leaves without spots or discolouration
Strong upright stems with no lesions
Flowers present at appropriate growth stage
Fruit developing normally without blemishes
causes: No disease detected. Plant is growing under good conditions.

organic: Continue regular watering at the base of plants
Apply compost or organic fertiliser to maintain soil health
Monitor weekly for early signs of pest or disease
chemical: No treatment needed at this time
Consider preventive copper spray during rainy season as protection
prevention: Maintain current watering and fertilisation schedule
Keep the area around plants weed-free
Inspect plants twice weekly for early pest or disease signs
Rotate planting location next season

description: Common rust is a widespread fungal disease of maize (corn) that occurs in most maize-growing regions of the world. It is particularly prevalent in tropical highlands and can cause significant yield loss.
symptoms: Small, circular to elongated, cinnamon-brown powdery pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules rupture to release reddish-brown spores (urediniospores)
Heavily infected leaves turn yellow then die prematurely
Late-season infections may produce dark brown-black pustules (teliospores)
Severe infections reduce photosynthesis and grain fill
causes: Caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. Spreads through airborne spores over long distances. Favoured by cool to moderate temperatures (16â€“23Â°C) and high humidity or dew.

organic: Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves if infestation is early
Apply neem-based spray to slow spore germination
Ensure adequate potassium nutrition â€” potassium deficiency increases rust susceptibility
chemical: Apply Propiconazole (Tilt) or Tebuconazole fungicide at first sign of disease
Mancozeb as a protective spray before disease onset during high-risk periods
Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole (Headline AMP) for both protection and eradication
prevention: Plant rust-resistant maize hybrids â€” most modern varieties have good resistance
Plant early in the season to avoid peak rust conditions
Avoid planting at high altitude where cool, humid conditions favour rust
Scout fields regularly from tasselling stage onwards
Apply preventive fungicide sprays if 5% of plants show rust before tasselling

description: Northern leaf blight (NLB) is one of the most significant fungal diseases of maize globally. Under favourable conditions, it can cause 30â€“50% yield losses, making it a major threat to food security in Africa and Asia.
symptoms: Long, elliptical, cigar-shaped lesions 2.5â€“15cm in length
Lesions start as small, water-soaked spots that turn tan/grey with dark borders
Olive-green to black spore masses visible in the centre of mature lesions
Lesions begin on lower leaves and progress upward
Severe infections cause entire leaves to die, leaving only green midrib
causes: Caused by the fungus Exserohilum turcicum (formerly Helminthosporium turcicum). Survives on infected crop residue. Spreads via airborne conidia. Favoured by cool (18â€“27Â°C), wet weather with extended dew periods.

organic: Remove and burn crop debris after harvest to eliminate fungal source
Apply copper-based fungicide sprays at early disease stages
Foliar application of compost tea to boost plant immunity
chemical: Apply Propiconazole (Tilt) at VT (tasselling) stage as preventive spray
Azoxystrobin (Amistar) provides both preventive and curative activity
Pyraclostrobin + Metconazole (Headline) for high-value crops
prevention: Use certified NLB-resistant maize hybrids â€” this is the most effective control
Practice crop rotation â€” rotate with non-grass crops (legumes, vegetables)
Till crop residue into the soil after harvest to speed decomposition
Avoid dense plant populations â€” maintain recommended spacing
Apply preventive fungicide at silking stage during high-risk seasons

description: Early blight affects potato crops worldwide and is especially damaging in tropical and subtropical regions. It reduces photosynthetic area and weakens plants, leading to reduced tuber size and yield.
symptoms: Dark brown circular spots with concentric rings (bull's-eye pattern) on older leaves
Yellow (chlorotic) area surrounding the dark lesions
Lesions may coalesce, causing large areas of leaf tissue to die
Severe defoliation starting from the base of the plant upward
Dark, sunken, leathery lesions may appear on tubers at harvest
causes: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Survives on infected plant debris and soil. Spread by wind and rain splash. Favoured by warm temperatures (24â€“29Â°C) and alternating wet and dry conditions.

organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves â€” especially in early stages
Apply copper-based fungicide spray every 7â€“10 days
Spray Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) as a biological control agent
Improve soil fertility â€” healthy plants are more resistant
chemical: Chlorothalonil spray every 7 days from early tuber formation
Mancozeb + Metalaxyl (Ridomil) as a combined protection
Azoxystrobin (Amistar) for systemic protection â€” rotate with contact fungicides
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed tubers
Maintain adequate plant nutrition â€” especially potassium and calcium
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation which promotes lush, susceptible growth
Rotate crops â€” do not plant potatoes in the same field for 3 years
Harvest tubers promptly when mature to avoid tuber infection

description: Potato late blight is the most devastating crop disease in history â€” responsible for the Irish Famine of 1845â€“49. It remains the world's most destructive potato pathogen, causing losses of up to $6 billion annually.
symptoms: Water-soaked, dark green to brown lesions on leaf tips and margins
White, fluffy sporulation visible on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Infected stems show brown, water-soaked lesions that collapse
Tubers show reddish-brown discolouration under the skin extending into flesh
Entire fields can be destroyed within 7â€“10 days under ideal conditions
causes: Caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Survives in infected tubers left in the soil. Spreads rapidly via airborne spores (sporangia) in cool (10â€“20Â°C), wet conditions. New aggressive strains (Blue-13) are now common in Africa.

organic: Remove and burn all infected foliage immediately â€” never compost
Apply copper hydroxide (Kocide) every 5 days during wet weather
Destroy all infected tubers â€” they are a source for next season's disease
Spray Bacillus subtilis as a preventive biological treatment
chemical: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold MZ) â€” most effective systemic fungicide
Cymoxanil (Curzate) + Mancozeb contact combination
Fluopicolide (Infinito) for resistant strains â€” rotate modes of action every 2 sprays
prevention: Plant only certified, blight-free seed tubers â€” critical first step
Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Sarpo Mira, Cara, Valor) where available
Monitor Blitecast/Smith Period forecasts and spray before disease appears
Earth up plants to protect tubers from spore wash-down
Destroy all volunteer potato plants and cull piles that could harbour the disease

description: Rice blast is the most important disease of rice worldwide, causing 10â€“30% yield losses annually. In Africa and South Asia, it is a major cause of food insecurity. The pathogen can infect at all growth stages.
symptoms: Diamond-shaped lesions with grey or white centres and brown borders on leaves
Lesions may coalesce, killing entire leaves rapidly
Neck blast: infection at the panicle neck causes the panicle to break (rotten neck)
Collar blast: lesion at the base of the leaf blade
Severely infected seedlings can die (damping off)
causes: Caused by the fungus Magnaporthe oryzae. Spreads through airborne conidia. Favoured by high humidity, temperatures of 24â€“28Â°C, and high nitrogen levels. Can spread explosively during prolonged dew or fog periods.

organic: Reduce nitrogen fertilisation â€” excessive nitrogen promotes blast susceptibility
Apply silicon fertiliser (silica) â€” improves plant resistance to blast
Spray Trichoderma harzianum biological fungicide as preventive treatment
Improve field drainage to reduce humidity
chemical: Apply Tricyclazole (Beam) at tillering and before heading â€” most effective
Isoprothiolane (Fuji-One) as protective and curative treatment
Azoxystrobin (Amistar) for neck blast prevention â€” apply 7 days before heading
prevention: Plant blast-resistant rice varieties (NERICA, IR64, Sahel 108 in Africa)
Use balanced fertilisation â€” split nitrogen applications, avoid excess
Avoid dense planting â€” maintain recommended row and plant spacing
Use certified disease-free seeds â€” treat seeds with fungicide before planting
Scout fields weekly from tillering stage and act at first sign of leaf blast

description: Cassava Mosaic Disease (CMD) is the most important disease constraint to cassava production in Africa. It causes losses estimated at over $1 billion annually, threatening food security for millions who depend on cassava as a staple food.
symptoms: Distinctive mosaic pattern of yellow-green and dark green patches on leaves
Leaf distortion, twisting, and reduced leaf size (stunting)
Severely affected plants have small, deformed, chlorotic leaves
Reduced stem elongation â€” plants appear stunted
Tubers of infected plants are smaller and reduced in number
causes: Caused by African Cassava Mosaic Virus (ACMV) and related begomoviruses. Transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci. Also spread through infected stem cuttings used as planting material. The Uganda Variant (UgV) is particularly severe.

organic: Remove and destroy severely infected plants immediately to reduce virus source
Control whitefly vectors using yellow sticky traps
Apply neem-based insecticide spray to control whitefly populations
Intercrop with cowpea or sweet potato to confuse whitefly navigation
chemical: Apply imidacloprid (Confidor) systemic insecticide to control whitefly vectors
Use thiamethoxam (Actara) as soil drench or foliar spray for whitefly control
Note: no direct cure for virus â€” vector control is the primary chemical strategy
prevention: CRITICAL: Use only CMD-resistant or tolerant varieties (e.g., TME 419, TMS 30572, IITA varieties)
Source planting material only from certified disease-free nurseries
Rogue (remove) infected plants early in the growing season
Control whitefly populations in the field from planting time
Avoid replanting cuttings from infected fields

description: Bacterial spot is one of the most serious diseases of pepper in tropical and subtropical regions. It affects all above-ground parts of the plant and can cause severe defoliation and significant fruit loss.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown with yellow halo
Spots may have a greasy appearance and irregular shape
Severe defoliation leaving plants bare and fruit exposed to sunscald
Raised, brown, scabby spots on fruit surface that crack open
Dark, water-soaked lesions on stems that turn tan with age
causes: Caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria bacteria. Spread by rain splash, contaminated tools, and infected transplants. Survives on seed and plant debris. Favoured by warm (24â€“30Â°C), wet conditions.

organic: Remove infected leaves and plant parts â€” dispose away from the field
Apply copper sulphate or copper hydroxide spray every 5â€“7 days
Avoid working with plants when wet to prevent mechanical spread
Use garlic or chilli extract spray as a natural antibacterial
chemical: Copper hydroxide + Mancozeb combination every 5 days
Streptomycin sulphate (where permitted) for severe infections
Kasugamycin (Kasumin) antibiotic spray as an alternative
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants from reputable nurseries
Treat seeds with hot water (50Â°C for 25 minutes) before planting
Avoid overhead irrigation â€” use drip irrigation at plant base
Rotate crops â€” do not plant pepper or tomato in same area for 2â€“3 years
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach between plants

description: Yellow rust (also called stripe rust) is one of the three major wheat rust diseases. It has a global distribution and is particularly devastating in cool, moist environments including highland East Africa and South Asia. New aggressive races have emerged threatening previously resistant varieties.
symptoms: Bright yellow-orange pustules arranged in stripes along the leaf veins
Pustules are small and powdery, releasing yellow spores when rubbed
Yellow stripes visible from a distance â€” gives fields a yellowish appearance
Severely infected leaves turn brown and die prematurely
On susceptible varieties, entire flag leaf may be covered with pustules
causes: Caused by the fungus Puccinia striiformis. Spreads through wind-borne urediniospores over hundreds of kilometres. Favoured by cool temperatures (7â€“12Â°C) and high moisture. Aggressive new races (e.g., Warrior, Digby) are overcoming resistance.

organic: Remove and destroy infected plant material where feasible on small plots
Apply potassium silicate foliar spray to strengthen plant cell walls
Ensure balanced fertilisation â€” avoid excess nitrogen
chemical: Propiconazole (Tilt 250 EC) â€” most commonly used and effective
Tebuconazole + Trifloxystrobin (Nativo) for both protection and eradication
Epoxiconazole (Opus) â€” apply at first sign of disease for best results
prevention: Plant rust-resistant wheat varieties â€” consult your national agricultural research institute
Monitor fields weekly during cool, moist weather periods
Plant early or late to avoid peak rust conditions
Apply preventive fungicide at flag leaf emergence (GS 39-45) in high-risk seasons
Subscribe to rust monitoring alerts from CIMMYT or national authorities

description: Septoria leaf spot is one of the most destructive diseases of tomato foliage. It rarely affects fruit directly but causes severe defoliation, weakening the plant and exposing fruit to sunscald.
symptoms: Numerous small circular spots (3-5mm) with dark brown borders and grey-white centres
Small black dots (pycnidia) visible in the centre of each spot
Lower leaves affected first, disease moves upward rapidly
Heavy infections cause leaves to yellow and drop
Defoliation leads to fruit sunscald
causes: Caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. Survives on infected plant debris. Spreads by rain splash and wind. Favoured by warm temperatures (20-25C) and high humidity.

organic: Remove and destroy all infected lower leaves immediately
Apply copper-based fungicide every 7-10 days
Spray neem oil solution to slow spore germination
Mulch around plant base to prevent soil splash
chemical: Chlorothalonil (Daconil) spray every 7 days
Mancozeb as a protective treatment before wet periods
Azoxystrobin for systemic protection of new growth
prevention: Rotate crops — do not grow tomatoes in same spot for 2-3 years
Water at the base of plants only
Remove all plant debris at end of season
Space plants 60cm apart for good air circulation

description: Two-spotted spider mites are tiny arachnids that feed on the underside of tomato leaves. They build damaging populations very quickly in hot, dry weather.
symptoms: Fine stippling (tiny yellow or white dots) on upper leaf surface
Fine silky webbing on the underside of leaves and between stems
Leaves turn bronze, yellow, then brown and die
Tiny moving dots (0.5mm) visible on leaf underside — the mites themselves
Severe infestations cause complete defoliation
causes: Caused by the mite Tetranychus urticae. Thrives in hot (above 27C), dry conditions. Spreads by wind, clothing, and tools. Pesticide overuse destroys natural predators, causing outbreaks.

organic: Spray plants forcefully with water to knock mites off leaves
Apply neem oil spray (2% solution) every 5-7 days
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) as biological control
Spray insecticidal soap on leaf undersides
chemical: Abamectin (Agrimec) miticide — effective against all life stages
Spiromesifen (Oberon) for resistance management
Rotate miticides — mites develop resistance very quickly
prevention: Keep plants well-watered — stressed plants are more susceptible
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation
Monitor plants weekly in hot, dry weather
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators

description: Target spot is an emerging fungal disease of tomatoes increasingly important in tropical and subtropical regions. It affects leaves, stems, and fruit.
symptoms: Brown spots with concentric rings giving a bull-eye appearance
Spots have yellow halos surrounding the lesion
Lesions appear on leaves, stems, and fruit
Dark brown fruit lesions that are slightly sunken
Severe leaf infections cause yellowing and defoliation
causes: Caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola. Favoured by warm (20-30C), humid conditions. Spreads through airborne spores and infected plant material.

organic: Remove infected plant material promptly
Apply copper hydroxide spray every 7-10 days
Improve air circulation around plants by pruning
chemical: Azoxystrobin (Amistar) provides good systemic control
Boscalid + Pyraclostrobin (Pristine) for difficult infections
Tebuconazole as an alternative mode of action
prevention: Use disease-free transplants from certified nurseries
Rotate crops annually
Avoid overhead irrigation
Clean up all plant debris after harvest

description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is one of the most devastating diseases of tomatoes in tropical and subtropical regions. Early infections can destroy entire crops. It is transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly.
symptoms: Young leaves curl upward and inward, becoming cup-shaped
Leaves turn yellow (chlorotic) between the veins
Severely stunted plant growth
Flowers drop before fruit forms, leading to near-zero yield
Older leaves may appear leathery and thickened
causes: Caused by Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV). Transmitted exclusively by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Spreads rapidly when whitefly populations are high.

organic: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whitefly using yellow sticky traps
Apply neem oil or insecticidal soap to reduce whitefly
Use reflective silver mulch to disorient whiteflies
chemical: Apply imidacloprid (Confidor) to control whitefly vectors
Thiamethoxam (Actara) as soil drench or foliar spray
No cure for the virus — vector control is the only strategy
prevention: CRITICAL: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties (e.g., Tygress, Shanty)
Use insect-proof netting to exclude whiteflies in nurseries
Remove infected plants within 24 hours of detection
Control weeds that host whiteflies around the field

description: Tomato mosaic virus is a highly stable plant virus that persists in soil and plant debris for years. It spreads mechanically during pruning and field operations.
symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves
Leaf distortion — leaves may appear fern-like or crinkled
Stunted plant growth in early-infected plants
Fruit may show internal browning or uneven ripening
Yellow mottling on leaves
causes: Caused by Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV). Spreads through mechanical contact — infected sap on hands, tools, or clothing. No insect vector. Seed transmission can occur.

organic: Remove and destroy infected plants — do not compost
Wash hands thoroughly with soap between plants
No cure — management focuses on removing infected plants
chemical: No effective chemical treatment exists for the virus
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach or 70% alcohol
Treat seeds with trisodium phosphate (TSP) solution
prevention: Use certified virus-free seed and transplants
Plant resistant varieties (Tm-2 or Tm-2^2 resistance gene)
Disinfect all tools between plants during pruning
Avoid smoking near plants
Wash hands before entering the growing area

description: Gray leaf spot (GLS) is the most yield-limiting foliar disease of maize globally. Under severe conditions it can cause 50%+ yield loss. Most destructive in humid climates with reduced tillage.
symptoms: Long, narrow rectangular lesions running parallel between leaf veins
Lesions initially appear as small tan spots with yellow halos
Mature lesions are grey to tan, 1-6cm long, with distinct parallel edges
Heavy infections cause large areas of leaf tissue to die
Grey sporulation visible on lesion surface in humid conditions
causes: Caused by the fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis. Survives on infected corn residue. Spores spread by wind and splash. Favoured by warm (25-30C), humid conditions with prolonged dew periods.

organic: Till crop residue into soil after harvest
Improve air circulation through proper plant spacing
Apply balanced fertilisation — especially potassium
chemical: Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole (Headline AMP) applied at tasselling
Pyraclostrobin (Headline) for high-value crops
Propiconazole (Tilt) as a cost-effective option
prevention: Plant GLS-resistant hybrids — most effective control measure
Rotate crops with non-grass species (soybean, vegetables)
Till crop debris to reduce inoculum
Apply preventive fungicide at VT in high-risk years

description: Your maize plant appears healthy! No signs of disease detected. Continue your current crop management practices.
symptoms: Deep green leaves without spots or lesions
Strong upright stalks with no discolouration
Well-formed tassels and silks
Cobs developing normally
causes: No disease detected. Plant is in good condition.

organic: Continue regular watering and fertilisation
Scout weekly for pests and diseases
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Rotate crops next season
Keep field free of weeds

description: Your potato plant appears healthy! No disease detected. Keep up your good management practices.
symptoms: Lush, dark green compound leaves without spots
Strong stems without discolouration
Flowers present at appropriate growth stage
Tubers forming normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Continue current watering and hilling practices
Monitor weekly for late blight in wet weather
chemical: No treatment needed
Apply preventive copper spray before expected wet periods
prevention: Keep hilling soil around stems
Rotate location next season
Harvest at full maturity

description: Your pepper plant appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Dark green, glossy leaves without spots
Strong stems without lesions
Flowers or developing fruit with no blemishes
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Maintain regular watering schedule
Monitor weekly for pests and disease
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation
Maintain good air circulation
Rotate crops next season

description: Apple scab is the most economically important disease of apples worldwide. Without management, it can cause near total crop loss in susceptible varieties during wet spring seasons.
symptoms: Olive-green to brown, velvety or scab-like lesions on leaf surfaces
Infected leaves may yellow and drop prematurely
Dark, rough, scab-like lesions on fruit surface
Fruit lesions crack and allow entry of secondary rots
Severely affected fruit is unmarketable
causes: Caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. Overwinters in infected fallen leaves. Releases spores during spring rains when temperatures are above 6C.

organic: Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
Apply copper or sulphur fungicide at bud break
Apply lime sulphur spray during dormant season
chemical: Apply Captan from green tip stage every 7-14 days
Myclobutanil (Rally) for post-infection curative control within 72 hours
Trifloxystrobin (Flint) for both protective and curative activity
prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties (e.g., Liberty, Freedom, Redfree)
Prune trees to open the canopy
Use a predictive spray programme
Mow and remove fallen leaves in autumn
Avoid overhead irrigation during critical infection period

description: Apple black rot affects fruit, leaves, and bark. Infected fruit becomes mummified and hangs on the tree as a source of inoculum for future seasons.
symptoms: Purple spots on leaves that enlarge to form frog-eye lesions with brown centres
Fruit rot beginning as small purple spots, expanding to large black rot
Infected fruit becomes mummified — shrivelled, black, and hard
Dark, sunken cankers on limbs and trunk
Cankers can girdle branches causing dieback
causes: Caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. Survives in mummified fruit, dead bark, and cankers. Spreads by rain-splashed spores.

organic: Remove all mummified fruit and dead wood immediately
Apply copper fungicide sprays from pink stage
Paint wound dressings on pruning cuts
chemical: Captan sprays from pink through cover
Thiophanate-methyl for canker control
Myclobutanil during summer months
prevention: Prune out all dead and cankered wood during dormancy
Remove mummified fruit — primary inoculum source
Avoid tree stress through proper nutrition and irrigation
Protect trees from fire blight wounds

description: Cedar apple rust requires two different host plants to complete its life cycle — apple trees and Eastern red cedar. It causes bright orange spots on apple leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots with slightly raised centres on upper leaf surface
Small tube-like structures (aecia) on the underside of leaf spots
Lesions on fruit and young stems in severe infections
Early defoliation in heavily infected trees
Orange gelatinous horns visible on nearby juniper trees in spring
causes: Caused by the fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. Alternates between Eastern red cedar/juniper and apple. Spores released from cedar galls during wet spring weather infect apple.

organic: Remove nearby Eastern red cedar trees if feasible
Apply sulphur fungicide at petal fall stage
Apply copper spray at bud break as preventive
chemical: Myclobutanil (Rally) or Trifloxystrobin from pink stage
Apply at 7-10 day intervals from pink through 3rd cover spray
Propiconazole as an alternative fungicide
prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties (e.g., Liberty, Redfree)
Remove Eastern red cedar trees within 300m if possible
Apply protective fungicides starting at bud break

description: Your apple tree appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Dark green, glossy leaves without spots or distortion
Clean fruit developing without blemishes
Healthy bark without cankers
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Maintain regular pruning for good air circulation
Monitor monthly for pests and disease
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Keep orchard floor clean of fallen fruit and leaves
Prune annually during dormancy

description: Grape black rot is one of the most destructive diseases of grapes in humid climates. Under favourable conditions, entire clusters can be lost. Infected berries shrivel into hard, black mummies.
symptoms: Small tan spots with dark brown margins on leaves
Black pimple-like structures (pycnidia) in the centre of leaf lesions
Infected berries turn brown then rapidly shrivel and turn black
Black mummified berries remain attached to cluster
Brown elongated lesions on young shoots and tendrils
causes: Caused by the fungus Guignardia bidwellii. Overwinters in mummified berries and infected canes. Critical infection period is from bloom to 3-4 weeks after bloom.

organic: Remove all mummified berries
Apply copper or sulphur fungicide from bud break through 4-6 weeks post-bloom
Remove and destroy infected clusters immediately
chemical: Myclobutanil (Rally) from pre-bloom through cover sprays
Tebuconazole (Elite) for systemic curative control
Captan as a broad-spectrum protective spray
prevention: Remove all mummified fruit and infected canes during winter pruning
Train vines to improve air circulation
Apply protective fungicide sprays from bud break

description: Esca (Black Measles) is a complex grapevine trunk disease causing chronic decline and sudden vine death. It has become increasingly important worldwide.
symptoms: Interveinal yellowing (white grapes) or reddening (red grapes) in a tiger-stripe pattern
Berries develop small dark spots with purple borders — the black measles symptom
Internal wood shows dark streaking when canes are cut
Sudden wilting and death of entire vine in hot weather (apoplexy)
Annual recurrence of foliar symptoms with progressive decline
causes: Caused by a complex of wood-rotting fungi. Enters through pruning wounds. Spreads through infected planting material and pruning tools.

organic: Paint all pruning cuts with Trichoderma wound sealant
Remove severely affected vines and replant
Use double pruning technique to minimise wound size
chemical: No fully effective curative treatment exists
Apply wound protectant (Thiophanate-methyl) immediately after pruning
Trunk surgery to remove infected wood
prevention: Prune during dry weather to reduce spore dispersal
Apply wound protectant immediately after every pruning cut
Disinfect pruning tools with 10% bleach between vines
Source certified clean planting material

description: Grape leaf blight causes irregular brown lesions on leaves and defoliation. It typically appears later in the season.
symptoms: Large irregular dark brown lesions on leaf margins and between veins
Lesions may have a darker brown border with lighter brown interior
Infected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
Lesions on fruit stems can cause berry drop
causes: Caused by the fungus Isariopsis clavispora. Favoured by warm, humid conditions. Often occurs as a secondary disease.

organic: Remove infected leaves to reduce inoculum
Apply copper-based fungicide spray
Improve canopy management to increase air flow
chemical: Mancozeb as protective spray
Myclobutanil for systemic control
Apply during routine mildew spray programme
prevention: Maintain open canopy through leaf removal
Apply routine fungicide programme from bud break
Avoid overhead irrigation

description: Your grapevine appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Bright green undistorted leaves without spots
Clean shoot growth
Berries developing uniformly
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Continue routine canopy management
Monitor fortnightly for disease and pests
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Maintain open canopy for good air circulation
Scout regularly throughout the growing season

description: Strawberry leaf scorch weakens plants by reducing photosynthetic area. Severe infections can kill plants and reduce runner production significantly.
symptoms: Small irregular purple to dark red spots on upper leaf surface
Spots coalesce giving the leaf a scorched or burnt appearance
Heavily infected leaves turn brown-purple and die
Lesions may appear on petioles, runners, and fruit stalks
Plant vigour and yield reduced in chronic infections
causes: Caused by the fungus Diplocarpon earlianum. Survives on infected plant debris. Spreads by rain splash. Favoured by wet conditions and mild temperatures (15-25C).

organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves
Apply copper-based fungicide every 7-10 days
Improve drainage and air circulation around plants
chemical: Captan spray every 7-14 days during wet weather
Myclobutanil for systemic control
Pyraclostrobin for curative and protective activity
prevention: Plant disease-resistant varieties
Renovate strawberry beds annually after fruiting
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove all old leaves and debris after harvest

description: Your strawberry plant appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Dark green, undistorted leaves without spots
Healthy runners and crowns
Fruit developing normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Maintain regular watering
Apply straw mulch to keep fruit clean
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Renovate bed annually
Rotate planting site every 3-4 years

description: Powdery mildew is a common and disfiguring disease of sweet and sour cherries, causing significant losses in young orchards and affecting fruit quality.
symptoms: White powdery coating on leaf surfaces, shoots, and young fruit
Infected leaves curl, pucker, and become distorted
Affected shoots are stunted and may die back
Fruit develops a rough, russeted appearance
Defoliation of infected shoots late in the season
causes: Caused by the fungus Podosphaera clandestina. Thrives in warm (20-27C) conditions with high humidity at night. Does not need free water to infect.

organic: Apply potassium bicarbonate spray — disrupts fungal cell walls
Spray neem oil every 7 days
Apply sulphur fungicide (not above 32C)
chemical: Myclobutanil (Rally) as systemic curative treatment
Trifloxystrobin (Flint) for protective and curative activity
Tebuconazole as alternative — rotate to prevent resistance
prevention: Plant resistant cherry varieties where available
Apply preventive fungicide from pink stage of bud development
Prune to maintain open canopy
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation

description: Your cherry plant appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Deep green glossy leaves without distortion
Healthy shoot growth
Fruit developing normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Maintain regular monitoring
Apply balanced fertilisation
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Continue regular orchard hygiene
Prune annually for good air circulation

description: Powdery mildew is the most common disease of cucurbits (squash, pumpkin, cucumber, melon). It reduces yield and quality and causes premature senescence.
symptoms: White to light grey powdery spots on upper and lower leaf surfaces
Spots enlarge and merge, eventually covering the entire leaf
Infected leaves yellow, brown, and die prematurely
Stems and petioles may also be infected
Fruit smaller due to reduced photosynthesis
causes: Caused by the fungus Podosphaera xanthii. Thrives in warm (20-30C) conditions with high humidity at night but dry days. Spreads rapidly through airborne spores.

organic: Apply potassium bicarbonate spray (2 tbsp per litre) — very effective
Spray neem oil every 7 days on both leaf surfaces
Apply diluted milk spray (40% milk, 60% water)
chemical: Myclobutanil systemic fungicide for established infections
Azoxystrobin for both protection and eradication
Sulphur (avoid above 32C)
prevention: Plant resistant squash varieties
Ensure adequate plant spacing
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation
Remove and destroy infected leaves early

description: Citrus greening (Huanglongbing/HLB) is the most destructive citrus disease in the world. There is no cure. It has devastated citrus industries across Asia, Africa, and the Americas.
symptoms: Asymmetric yellowing (blotchy mottle) of leaves
Shoots with small, upright, yellow leaves
Small, misshapen, bitter fruit that remains green at maturity
Fruit drops prematurely and seeds abort
Progressive dieback of branches; eventual death of tree
causes: Caused by the bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus. Transmitted by the Asian citrus psyllid insect (Diaphorina citri). Also spreads through infected budwood.

organic: Remove and destroy all infected trees immediately
Control psyllid vector using organic-approved insecticides
No cure — removal is the only management option
chemical: Control Asian citrus psyllid with imidacloprid soil drench
Thiamethoxam (Actara) for vector control
Nutritional sprays (zinc, micronutrients) can slow decline but do not cure
prevention: Source certified HLB-free planting material ONLY
Install insect-proof screens in nurseries
Monitor trees monthly for psyllid insects
Report any suspect trees to your extension officer immediately

description: Bacterial spot is one of the most serious diseases of peaches and nectarines, causing significant economic losses in warm, humid growing regions.
symptoms: Small water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown with purple margins and yellow halos
Infected leaf spots fall out leaving a shot-hole appearance
Heavily infected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
Small circular dark water-soaked spots on fruit that become sunken and cracked
Dark elongated lesions on young twigs
causes: Caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni bacteria. Survives in infected twigs and buds. Spreads by wind-driven rain during spring.

organic: Apply copper hydroxide sprays every 5-7 days
Avoid working in the orchard when trees are wet
Remove mummified fruit and infected twigs during dormant pruning
chemical: Copper hydroxide + Mancozeb combination during wet periods
Oxytetracycline antibiotic spray (where permitted)
Apply protective sprays before expected rain events
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Redhaven, Reliance, Contender)
Prune for good air circulation
Avoid low-lying sites with poor air drainage
Apply dormant copper spray before bud swell

description: Your peach tree appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Dark green undistorted leaves without spots
Clean shoot growth
Fruit developing normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Maintain regular irrigation
Monitor monthly for pests and disease
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Continue dormant pruning programme
Apply dormant copper spray annually

description: Your soybean plant appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Trifoliate leaves are dark green and undistorted
Stem shows no lesions
Pods developing normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
Maintain soil health
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Rotate with non-legume crops next season
Use certified disease-free seed

description: Your raspberry plant appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Pinnate leaves are dark green without spots
Canes are vigorous without lesions
Fruit developing normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Continue regular watering and fertilisation
Tie canes to supports for good air circulation
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Remove old fruited canes after harvest
Rotate planting site every 5 years

description: Your blueberry plant appears healthy! No disease detected.
symptoms: Small oval leaves are green and healthy
Stems are reddish and vigorous
Berries developing normally
causes: No disease detected.

organic: Maintain soil pH at 4.5-5.5
Apply acidic mulch (pine bark, sawdust)
Monitor for mummy berry and other diseases
chemical: No treatment needed
prevention: Maintain correct soil pH
Prune annually for good air circulation
